summary: On-require self-updater fetches and executes unpinned tarball from author's personal GitHub branch
details: The package's main entry (index.js, ~15 MB, heavily obfuscated with obfuscator.io string-array + control-flow flattening) executes a top-level async IIFE on every require(). The IIFE queries https://registry.npmjs.org/utils-mf for dist-tags.latest, and on any version mismatch with the local package.json fetches https://github.com/ruhend2001/maleficent/.../utils-mf-<version>.tgz from the author's personal GitHub repository (mutable main-branch path, not a pinned commit SHA), writes it to./tmp/upgrade.tgz, and runs `exec('tar -xzf./tmp/upgrade.tgz -C./node_modules/utils-mf')` to overwrite the installed package in place. The fetched bytes are unpinned, unverified (no hash/signature check), opaque (same obfuscated bundle), and become the code loaded on the next require. Whatever the author pushes to that GitHub branch in the future will execute on every consumer's machine without a new npm install. The package metadata (`description: "utility mf"`) misleadingly understates the shipped behavior — the actual code is a WhatsApp Baileys bot framework with GitHub/GitLab token-based DB backup and media-scraping helpers, concealed behind heavy obfuscation. The combination of mutable-source auto-update, opaque payload, obfuscation hiding the update path, and metadata mismatch is the dropper fingerprint.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
